speaker: interviewee
text: --and-- and the Nazis had on them. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: --my dealings with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Picking up where we left off, do you remember that night in the hotel? You said that your mother-- you know, not-- not a very good hotel, your mother told you, don't take your clothes off. Did you stay in the hotel? You didn't venture outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, we didn't-- we didn't venture out. We stayed in the hotel until the next day. I don't recall where we ate, but I think we may have gone downstairs. They may have had a little-- I don't recall, no. But I remember that we definitely did not go out because it was dangerous. Hamburg was not a safe city, especially that area was not safe. And then the next day, I remember we went to the dock very early and got on the ship very early. And they examined our-- our two suitcases and made a demand as to how much money we were taking out. And because of what had happened to my father, my mother was very careful about what she was bringing out monetarily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember your feelings as you were walking toward the ship getting ready to get on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was amazed first of all that this was such a huge, huge ship. You know, for me it was looking up and it was-- it was just-- it was just immense. And then giving up our baggage for them to search it. And they went through everything. And then they took the bag-- we did not carry the bags up to the ship. The porters took it in. And then going up the gangplank and getting into our stateroom and then there-- it was a beautiful state room. It was-- was just lovely. And seeing what this ship was like, you know, having absolutely no freedom and then all of a sudden my mother said, well, you can walk around now. It's-- it's safe aboard the ship. And then as soon as we left, my mother and grandmother and sister got seasick so I was really left to my own devices. It was rather interesting because there was so much food aboard the ship that it was unbelievable. I mean, first class food. And I couldn't cut my own meat because, you know, I was a little boy, and so the waiter-- and they were very, very nice people-- the waiter cut my food for me and I could eat it then. And my mother could not-- never leave the stateroom because of sickness. And she-- she warned me, don't you go down-- in your wanderings around the ship, don't you go down to the engine room. Well, naturally the first place I headed was the engine room. And I was intrigued by the machinery. The engine room was where all of the die-hard Nazis were working. And she didn't-- she-- I guess she must have known that because-- but they were-- they didn't bother me. I mean, I remember walking around and looking down. They had like a little catwalk where you could stand and look down on-- on all the moving parts. The pistons and the piston rods were moving and I was just intrigued by this whole thing. And I went all over the ship all by myself. It was fascinating. And then once we put to sea, it was even more interesting because I had-- I had so much freedom. I had never tasted freedom like that before in my whole life.

speaker: interviewer
text: The hardcore Nazis, did they sing songs? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Yes, they sang songs. And I guess-- I never saw this, but I guess in certain cases, they made life a little bit rough on some of the people. They, you know, they'd give this heil Hitler salute and, you know, just to bring back memories to-- to these Jews. There were 937 Jews aboard the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember hearing them sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do remember them walking around and-- most of the people, the waiters and the busboys and the cleaning people, were very, very nice and very, very kind people towards us. So they were not caught up with this-- this Nazism.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that they were hardcore Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was told this. I was told this later on. So I-- at that time, I didn't know. I did not talk to the people, I just stood up on this catwalk and looked down and watched-- watched them work and watched the machinery in action.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just backing up, when you got on the ship, were you aware of the other passengers getting on, anything that you remember about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember-- because once we got settled in the stateroom and I-- I went up to the-- to the railing and looked down and watched the other passengers come aboard. And some of them were dressed in-- in, like, winter clothing. And I thought, gee, this is-- it isn't that cold. I remember thinking that. But outside of that, that was about it. I-- I just thought that it was strange that they were dressed so warm, like-- and I knew we were-- my mother had told me that we were going to a-- to an island which was very hot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so therefore, I mean, in-- in retrospect, what's your conclusion? You know, here they-- here they were in May.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were trying to bring as much clothing with them with the prospect of eventually coming to this country and anything that they couldn't stuff into their suitcase, they were wearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on board ship, when you were left to your own devices, did you-- did you get to know any of the other children? What was it like, playing games during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We played games. We had horse racing game. There was a swimming pool that they made. There was a game room. They even showed movies. They had-- they had a lot of things to do for the kids. A lot of the people were not seasick, so there were a lot of people out and they had these-- these-- these deck chairs and you could sit there and you could get-- they would serve bouillon. I remember drinking some bouillon and eating crackers. And I'd check up on my mother and grandmother and sister, oh, maybe two or three times a day to see- see how they were doing. And they were moaning and groaning down-- down in the cabin. They weren't happy at all. But they-- my sister still gets motion sickness.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a story that's told that some of the kids went-- went up and down the-- the hallways, soaping handles to staterooms.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know about that. I wasn't one of them. [LAUGHS] I didn't do that. I-- I do remember finding a toy and-- and throwing it overboard and thinking that, I wonder if anybody will ever find this before it sinks. But that's-- I think that's the only mischievous thing I did. It was a little toy wooden mallet. But I didn't know about the soaping, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet the captain, were you aware of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- yes, I was aware of him. And I saw him walking, but I-- he's not going to bother with a seven-year-old child, you know. He was not a very tall man, I-- I remember that. He looked very distinguished. But so did the other officers on the ship. But no, I had no dealings with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to any of the religious services on board?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I did look in on the religious service. I wanted to see what-- what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was just that I-- I went in. It was in in one of the-- one of the rooms. It was not in the main hall, but it was in one of the side rooms. And it was a morning service. And I did not count toward the quorum because I hadn't been bar mitzvahed. At that point, I was only seven years old or maybe-- I had-- did celebrate my eighth birthday on the ship. But-- --I just remember them standing up and praying and wearing the prayer shawls and the yarmulkes. But I-- I didn't stay very long and I-- and I left. But I do remember them doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any-- any Nazi insignia, pictures of the Fuhrer?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a large picture of Adolf Hitler in the main dining hall. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --I know that they took the picture down. I didn't see it, but I had heard that they did take the picture down when they had the Friday evening services or Saturday morning services. And then right after that, they put it back up again. And I know they did have some dancers in there. I-- I remember hearing the music and peeking in and seeing what was going on. But again, I was a little boy so I didn't feel like I belonged there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The trip lasted how long and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was 40 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not the trip to Havana.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. No, the trip to Havana, I-- I believe, was something like 13 days, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just in-- between-- between Germany and reaching Havana, anything else in terms of conversations with people, a sense of what lay ahead, life on board ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, life aboard ship was extremely pleasant. It was-- it was just-- for me, it was an absolutely magnificent way to live. And I thought, wow, this is really great. I remember going by the Azores and seeing-- seeing this island way off in the distance. And the flying fish totally intrigued me. I watched them for a long time. And the iridescence of the-- at night of the ship wake, that was fascinating. And then we had a funeral. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about the funeral.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well this elderly teacher died and-- of natural causes. And they had the funeral and it was-- it was after dark, but they had the searchlights playing on the-- on the body. The body was mounted on a plank and it was tilted-- --and they had some prayers. But it was-- the body was covered by the Nazi flag. And it was tilted on this board and then when the prayers were done-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --the body was in a bag and the bag was dropped into the ocean and then it just about immediately sank. So they must have had weights on it. And then it was over and plunk, he was gone. And they folded up the Nazi flag and pulled the plank in and that was it. And I thought, gee, it seems kind of a strange way to end someone's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: It took place at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you awake?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I had heard they were going to have this funeral, so I was-- I wanted to see this thing. It wasn't really that late at night. It wasn't like-- it was like maybe 9:30, ten o'clock, I thought. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it common knowledge that there--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. People were invited to-- to come to-- to the service. It was announced over the PA system, if I recall. That's how I knew it was. Because I mean, you couldn't read in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got closer to Havana, a sense of anticipation aboard ship? What-- what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember wondering what this place was going to be like because my mother told me it was going to be warm. And this-- and the weather started to get quite warm. In fact, it got hot. And once we got there, there was a tremendous amount of anticipation what was going on. But then they heard-- we heard that they weren't going to let us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you hear about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were already in the harbor. We had gone into the harbor. We were sitting in the harbor and then there was talk that there was some irregularity and they weren't going to let us in or they-- there was also talk that they wanted more money. Well, we had no more money and we couldn't figure out what was gonna happen to us. And we were sitting in the harbor and they had Cuban soldiers posted aboard the ship to prevent people from jumping ship. And we just stayed there. And then they had these little boats coming out and kids selling fruit. I can remember that, throwing the fruit aboard. And then my uncle from New Jersey flew down to Havana and rented one of those little boats and I remember seeing him. And now, since we were standing still, my mother and grandmother and sister were no longer seasick. And I didn't ask my mother if I could jump overboard to have my uncle rescue me and go with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did ask.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did ask, yes. And she said, no. Absolutely not. You're going to stay here. And so we stayed and we waited and waited. And while we were sitting there, there was a Cuban sergeant and I befriended him. I was intrigued by the rifle that he had and his uniform. And he wanted to adopt me and take me off the ship with him. And there's an interesting corollary to that. In 1980, I was stationed at Fort McCoy Wisconsin and we had 35,000 Cubans up there. And I was up there from Memorial Day to Labor Day. And I used to walk around and think, there but for the grace of God go I. I could have been on the other side of that-- that concertina wire fence except-- instead of being on this side. So it sort of came full circle.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but again, your mother said no to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother absolutely refused to let me jump into the water and-- and-- and also go with the Cuban sergeant. He would have taken me off, or sneaked me off, yeah. But she wasn't about to let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: But why would you have wanted to jump into the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: So that I could get off the ship because I-- I didn't know what was going to happen to us and I thought my uncle would more or less take me with him to Hackensack, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess my point is that at this particular time, you were already feeling that-- that things were-- were desperate.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Things were extremely desperate. And there must have been a lot of talk about you've got to take care of yourself and you have to do things to make sure that you survive. Otherwise, I wouldn't have gotten the crazy idea of jumping off that ship. There was somebody who did jump off and try to commit suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see it, do you remember it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I did not see it and I-- I only-- it was all hearsay that I heard and that he was picked up by the Cubans and was put in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because people who-- who did see it, in fact, tell stories about how he slit his wrists and how the-- the water was turned red with his blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: And that one of the people from did, in fact, jump in to save him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That's right. That's-- that's the story that I heard also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk about the people who you saw around you, what their reactions to essentially being in limbo were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terribly afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, the men who were in concentration camps were only released with the proviso that they would never return to Germany upon threat of death. So they were extremely upset that if they returned to Germany, they were going to get killed in a concentration camp. The women were deathly afraid that they were going to end up in a concentration camp also. So there was this tremendous anxiety and fear that we were not going to be able to enter Cuba. Where were we going to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then we heard we were going to the United States, that we were going to try and land there. And that sort of eased people's fears for a very short time frame. But their main concern was never, ever to go back to Germany. That was-- [SIGHS] --that was something that was almost unthinkable about doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just outside Havana harbor, in the harbor but still not-- not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not safe. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: --not at-- But not at land. You were still away from land, you weren't docked. You were in the harbor but not docked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We were-- we were in the harbor. And we were quite a ways out. I mean, it was-- it would have been a-- a good swimmer would have had to-- had to make it. A poor swimmer could not have made it. We were quite a ways out. I would say, well, it's hard for me to judge at that time, but it was quite a distance out. And they had these little boats, but the boats-- the little boats were close. And so I thought, hey, you know, somebody could jump overboard and get on one of those little boats and then-- not realizing you need the papers and that type of thing. I mean, I had no idea about those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some people were allowed to leave. Do you remember them leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were a couple, not very many. And I remember seeing them go down onto a tender and then going into Havana. And we all wondered, why are they the lucky ones? But I don't know-- I don't know how or why they were allowed to get off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody-- any extraordinary, beyond the man who tried to commit suicide and who jumped off, any extraordinary expressions of-- of anxiety, fear, anger, or hostility?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it that-- there wasn't much hostility, there wasn't too much anger. There was just tremendous fear. And I remember walking-- people walking around and talking. And some women were crying as to what are we going to do now, what's going to happen to us? And this-- this constant stress that they were living under was very difficult for them to cope.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother and your grandmother, what would they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once we departed again Havana harbor, they-- they were seasick and their immediate concern was fighting the seasickness. And I do recall my mother saying, things are-- don't worry-- telling me, don't worry, things are gonna be OK, things are going to work out. Now, she probably said that to alleviate the fears of a little boy. But I don't know how she personally felt, you know. And once we got to Miami, I said, well, here, we can come in this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever ask her afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- it was very difficult to talk to my mother about those times. She clammed up. She said she could not talk about it because it was too-- too emotionally draining for her. She couldn't talk-- she never told me about my father's death. I found that out from my cousin, Hans [? Achenhold ?] home from Kansas City. And my sister made a tape and my son heard the tape. I have not heard the tape. But that's the information I have was from that. I remember my father-- his death because there was a tremendous amount of crying and carrying on and I had no idea what was going on. And in Munich, my governess told me that my father was no longer alive, that he had died. But nobody-- and I thought he had been killed in a concentration camp for years and I did not know. My mother sort of let me believe that until I just recently found out about this tape and this conversation that my cousin, Hans, had with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So back on the going up to Miami, you remember when the ship left the harbor. I can't remember whether it was three or four days after you had-- or even longer-- that you-- that you were waiting outside of-- outside of Havana.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- it was probably closer to 10 days we were sitting in the harbor. And then-- then the captain tried to get us to Miami. And we got up to Miami and we saw-- and I remember seeing the automobiles on the causeway and looking at the-- at the hotels, the buildings. And I thought, wow, this is-- looks like a really nice place. And then the-- a Coast Guard cutter drove us off and we could not enter, not even get close enough so that somebody could jump in-- into the water and swim toward-- towards land. They just drove us right off and we had to turn right around and go back to Havana and sat there for a couple more-- well, a couple of more days. It was longer than a couple of days. It was quite a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a young boy on ship who was making-- he apparently had a compass and so he would be able to figure whether or not the ship was heading north or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apparently, there was-- there was a kid on board who was doing the calculations and so he would be able to tell from his-- from his figurings, so the story goes, which-- which way the ship was going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they did have-- in the main gangway or hallway, they did have a big map. And I remember seeing-- looking every day to see where we were, both when we left Hamburg to Havana and then when we were sitting down in-- in Havana watching the-- the plotting of the ship, which I guess every ship does. Where exactly-- where we were. And was enthralled with-- with the map, as well as our precise location.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the ship goes towards Miami, no possibility of the ship going to Miami or, for that matter, anywhere in the United States. Meanwhile, what-- what we're not talking about is all of the-- the negotiations, both on a-- an international scale, that were going on during this time, which you knew nothing about at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. We knew nothing. And it was scary. And I remember people talking about, what's gonna happen, what are we going to do? And then there was-- there was sort of a daily release of-- of information. But nobody really knew what was going to happen to us. No one had any concept of where we were going, what the future held in store, nothing. We were like in a-- in a void, in a vacuum. And tremendous anxiety in these people. They-- they even had squads walking-- of passengers walking around making sure that people were not planning to commit mass suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember them walking around, checking-- checking cabins. You know, because during-- this is-- during the day, you had to keep your cabin open so that they could check. And if you-- if they-- if you locked your cabin, then they would get a key and open it up to make sure that everything was OK. And I do remember them coming in the evening with a flashlight and checking to see if they-- if everyone was-- was still OK. But there was tremendous-- and they-- I remember the meetings that they would hold to discuss these things, to talk about what-- what were the possible causes of action.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are the passengers.

speaker: interviewee
text: The passengers, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a committee of the passengers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. And they-- they held meetings sometimes twice a day to-- to find out what-- what's going on. And then they would disseminate the news and-- and talk about it. But my mother, you know, she was so sick she-- [SIGHS] there was nothing-- she was more concerned about her sickness, her seasickness than anything else. And she felt everything was gonna be OK. But somehow, she got-- pulled herself together and got to the purser. And from-- from what I understand, she talked to the purser into getting us to go to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, explain how that was-- how that was possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I honestly don't know. She-- she claimed that-- that she talked to the purser and-- and convinced him that because that she had just her mother and two-- two-- two small children that she should be able to go to-- to England. And he did give us onto the thing. Whether she bribed him, I don't know. I don't know where she would get something to bribe him with. But she-- she did manage to get us to go to England. And she felt that the only safe place for us would be on the-- off the continent and-- and onto England itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what your quota numbers were for the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have no idea whether you were-- you were high on the list, one of the people who-- who would have been called sooner, rather than later, as in weeks or months, as opposed to a year or two?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I'm assuming because we got called in December of '39 that we were high on the list. Because we left in May of '39 and we were-- in June of '39 we were in Havana and six months later, our number came up. So I'm assuming from that information that we were high on the list.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the ship left Miami, went back to Cuba, at that point some-- some possibility that the-- the people aboard the were going to be allowed to go on the Island of Pines, which then never happened. Do you remember that part?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. That's interesting. I didn't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the ship decidedly turned--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and went back to the continent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. The ship was recalled by the owners, Hamburg America Line. And-- --then fear really set in. First of all, food became a scarce commodity. We could no longer-- had as much water as we wanted. The Cubans did allow us to refurbish the food stocks, but it was a decidedly different level of-- of food. But that's when fear really set in because we had no idea. We assumed at that time that we were going to be going to Hamburg, where we originally came from. Interestingly, we had bought round trip-- we had to buy round trip tickets, even though ostensibly it was going to be a-- a one-way trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so beyond a kind of rationing, the mood of the people--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was downcast-- --full of concerns as to what was going to happen-- --constant rumors-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: where were we going? No one really had any concept whatsoever of what was in the captain's mind. We knew that he was being recalled to Hamburg. Was the treatment by the staff any different? Were they-- were they still as conscientious, agreeable, attentive as before or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought so. I didn't think that it had changed much. I don't recall anyone being-- being any different on the trip back as to the trip going.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you explain the treatment by the-- by the crew in the first place?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the insistence of the captain. I think-- --crew people are used to following orders because a-- a ship is-- is only as good as its weakest member. And it was-- it's almost like a paramilitary organization. And the captain's word is law, and therefore they were going to obey the captain. The captain set up that these people are going to be treated decent and they're not going to be treated any different than any other cruise passenger. And he expected his people to comply. And they did, they followed orders. Germans are very good at following orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you listened in on those meetings by the-- by the passengers, the committee of the passengers, a group of I don't know, maybe--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of yelling, I remember that. I don't remember the exact terminology and the wordage that they used, but there was always a lot of yelling and-- and-- and screaming. And that's when I left. I said, you know, that isn't going to do any good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's time to change the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]
